title: Leverage Is a Double-Edged Sword
abstract: We use proprietary data on intraday transactions at a futures brokerage to analyze how implied leverage influences trading performance. Across all investors, leverage is negatively related to performance, due partly to increased trading costs and partly to forced liquidations resulting from margin calls. Defining skill out-of-sample, we find that relative performance differentials across unskilled and skilled investors persist. Unskilled investors' leverage amplifies losses from lottery preferences and the disposition effect. Leverage stimulates liquidity provision by skilled investors, and enhances returns. Although regulatory increases in required margins decrease skilled investors' returns, they enhance overall returns, and attenuate return volatility.

article.introduction:
The implicit leverageafforded by derivatives offers the prospect of magnified profits to investors. There are, however, several cautionary examples of investors who became financially distressed due to derivatives-induced losses. For instance, Archegos Capital Management lost $20 billion within two trading days due to a levered derivatives position (see Dahlquist, Sokolovski, and Sverdrup (2021)), Barings Bank was bankrupted by Nick Leeson's substantially levered position in derivatives (Rafeld, Fritz-Morgenthal, and Posch (2017)), and the oft-cited billion-plus dollar losses incurred by Metallgesellschaft and Robert Citron, the Orange County treasurer, involved derivatives bets.1Warnings that investors should avoid leveraged derivatives via discount brokerages such as Robinhood are commonplace in the popular press, although individual success stories are sometimes highlighted.2
  While there are many anecdotes surrounding derivatives leverage, some important questions deserve empirical investigation. For example, does the use of leverage benefit or hurt investors on average? Do there exist investors who can earn excess rents by using high leverage? What are the sources of leveraged profits and underperformance of investors stratified by skill? And, what is the role of forced liquidations due to margin calls, and investors' behavioral biases, in profits and losses due to leveraged positions? In this paper, we use high-frequency data to shed light on these questions. Notably, we measure skill during a training period to examine the leverage-performance link out-of-sample. At the outset, we clarify that our focus in this paper is not on spot leverage from borrowed funds, but rather on the leverage levels implied by the actual derivatives positions of traders. Thus, “leverage” here refers to the effective leverage implied by traders' marked-to-market positions in the contracts we consider.
  Our data set, kindly provided by a major futures broker in China, includes traded orders, intraday time-stamped transaction histories, day-end holdings, and account cash flows (injections and withdrawals) over a three-year period for more than 10,000 investors. While the majority of investors are individuals, some institutions are included as well. To the best of our knowledge, our paper is the first to analyze the interaction between traders' skill, leverage, and performance, and the mechanisms through which leverage impacts performance. Our principal metrics at the trader level are a daily rate of return (DRR) measure, the daily number of transactions (NTR), a daily turnover (DTO) measure, and the leverage level (DLV) implied by traders' nominal positions relative to the capital at stake.
  The futures investors we consider differ materially from the retail investors examined in most of the literature on equities. Based on a sample in the Chinese stock market between October 2018 and June 2019, Liu et al. (2022) report a mean monthly turnover of 35.5% and a median of 84.8%. Recent studies on Robinhood users show that half of them are first-time investors (Barber et al. (2022)), and that their portfolios display strong hysteresis (Welch (2022)), indicating moderate turnover. By contrast, futures investors are more experienced as per the entrance rules of the Chinese futures market,3and their mediandailyturnover exceeds 400% (see TableII), which is much higher than that in equities (Liu et al. (2022)). Given these differences, it is of particular interest to examine the relation between leverage and performance, as well as its underlying determinants.
  We construct two versions of DRR—gross and net of brokerage commissions. Across all traders, mean DRR (volatility) is negatively (positively) related to DLV, so that on average investors pay for leverage. After controlling for trading intensity, a one-unit increase in DLV is associated with a 7.94 basis points (bps) decrease in mean net DRR (about 19% annually) and a 0.73% increase in the daily standard deviation of net DRR (about 11% annually). Our results suggest that transaction costs account for about 23% of the negative relation between leverage and investors' net returns after accounting for trading frequency.
  Note, however, that beyond larger trading costs due to the larger scale of positions and persistently bad magnified bets, leverage can also be negatively related to DRR due to forced liquidations that arise from margin calls. We are not aware of previous studies that explicitly consider the second channel,4which our data allow us to examine. Forced liquidation can impact returns via the liquidity cost of the “fire sale” (or purchase). We find that for an average investor using a DLV of about 10 (similar to those in the highest quintile across all investors), the estimated liquidity cost due to forced liquidations accounts for 5.3% of their average gross DRR.5
  To identify skilled versus unskilled traders, we use a bootstrap procedure inspired by Fama and French (2010) and Harvey and Liu (2022). As in Ivković, Sialm, and Weisbenner (2008), Barber et al. (2009), Grinblatt, Keloharju, and Linnainmaa (2012), Barber et al. (2014), Che, Norli, and Priestley (2009), and Coval, Hirshleifer, and Shumway (2021), we find that performance varies considerably in the cross section. We identify groups of skilled and unskilled investors using data from the first sample year, and examine the performance of these groups out-of-sample. Although few investors demonstrate evidence of superior absolute skill out-of-sample, relative skill differentials do persist. Specifically, unskilled investors identified in-sample continue to underperform relative to skilled investors out-of-sample.6
  We explore the relation between skill, leverage, and performance in the out-of-sample period. We find that unskilled and skilled investors, on average, carry about the same amount of leverage. We further find, however, that conditional on skill, leverage is a double-edged sword for individual investors: The out-of-sample net returns of skilled (unskilled) investors are enhanced (decreased) by leverage. We analyze pivotal reasons for these findings. Existing research suggests that unskilled investors tend to exhibit a disposition effect (Odean (1998)), to trade on lottery characteristics such as skewness and volatility (Dorn, Dorn, and Sengmueller (2015), Luo and Subrahmanyam (2019), Liu et al. (2022)), and to trend-chase (Barberis et al. (2018)). In contrast, skilled investors tend to earn profits from informed trading (Black (1975)), de facto market making (Adrian, Etula, and Muir (2014)), and arbitrage activities (Hugonnier and Prieto (2015)). The question that arises is how leverage impacts these activities.
  For unskilled investors, we find supportive evidence that levered positions amplify unskilled investors' investment losses due to the tendency to trade on lottery characteristics as well as the disposition effect. For skilled investors, we find that these investors gain by using higher implied leverage: On average, a one-unit increase in leverage for skilled investors implies a 9.1 bps increase in net DRR (about 22% annually). We show that such investors' behavior is consistent with de facto market making, that is, intraday liquidity provision (see, for example, Grossman and Miller (1988) or Nagel (2012)). We also find that skilled investors engage in basis arbitrage. We do not find supportive evidence of informed trading by skilled investors at daily or longer horizons.
  The analyses above provide novel evidence on the relation between leverage and performance conditional on skill, but do not address causality. To better identify the economic pathway, we consider a regulatory change within our sample period that increased required margin ratios for some contracts. We then perform difference-in-differences (DiD) analyses of investors' behavior and performance around these increases. We find that highly levered investors prior to the regulatory change have the most significant decreases in leverage following the regulatory change relative to investors with low leverage; their net returns also increase relative to the other investors. The policy change is accompanied by modestly enhanced performance of unskilled investors and reduced profits of skilled investors. Overall, our identification scheme confirms the negative relationship between derivatives' implied leverage and investment returns.
  Our paper is closely related to two streams of literature. The first focuses on trading performance, and the other on leverage. The seminal works of Odean (1999), Barber and Odean (2000), Barber and Odean (2001), Barber et al. (2009), and Barber et al. (2022) study the effect of behavioral biases on investors' trading behavior and wealth. Using mutual fund liquidation data, Meyer and Pagel (2022) find that a considerable fraction of individuals forced to realize a loss dropped out the stock market. Bauer, Cosemans, and Eichholtz (2009) show that options trading is generally harmful to individual investors. Li, Subrahmanyam, and Yang (2018) show that issuers earn substantial profits by catering to investors' lottery preference via callable options. Xiong and Yu (2011), Cai et al. (2021), Pearson, Yang, and Zhang (2021), and Li, Subrahmanyam, and Yang (2021) study how market participants behave and perform in a warrants market bubble. Frazzini and Pedersen (2022) show that options and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) with high embedded leverage (which are, by definition, more risky) provide lower abnormal returns (alphas), suggesting they might be overpriced. We add to these papers by using trade-by-trade data to analyze how investors' skill interacts with implied leverage.
  Many theoretical papers analyze the effect of margin trading on asset price dynamics, and tackle the issue of how to regulate (manage) leverage to improve market quality (see, for example, Chowdhry and Nanda (1998), Brunnermeier and Pedersen (2009), Geanakoplos (2010), Garleanu and Pedersen (2011), Fostel and Geanakoplos (2012), Jacobs and Levy (2012), Jin and Zhou (2013), and Santos and Veronesi (2021)). Our analysis of cross-sectional dispersion in the performance of levered portfolios allows for additional avenues of future theoretical research.
  With respect to empirical work on leverage and margined trades, Hong, Kubik, and Fishman (2012) find that, after good earnings news, the price of highly shorted stocks (usually on margin) surges more compared with that of otherwise similar stocks. Using the unique features of the margin trading system in India, Kahraman and Tookes (2017) quantify the impact of trader leverage on market liquidity. They show that liquidity increases when stocks become eligible for margin trading, and that this relation reverses during crises.7Richardson, Saffi, and Sigurdsson (2017) find that securities with more leveraged investors tend to experience more extreme returns. Making use of publicly available data, Santa-Clara and Saretto (2009) show that margin requirements make the writing of put options less profitable. Bessembinder and Seguin (1993) analyze the links between volume and volatility in futures markets. Koudijs and Voth (2016) find that the bankruptcy of an investor consortium exposed lenders' activity and, following this bad experience, lenders increased the cost of leverage. Our high-frequency data at the account level allow us to contribute to this literature by explicitly considering the link between trader skill and leverage and the rationales for why leverage increases or reduces performance.
  In two important papers, Heimer and Simsek (2019) and Heimer and Imas (2022) analyze the impact of leverage restrictions on retail currency traders using DiD on a self-reported trader database at a social media website. Their analyses exploit the fact that U.S. retail traders' leverage was capped by regulation in 2010 while that of European traders was not. They focus on the effects of the leverage regulation on aggregate trader performance in the spot foreign exchange market. They show that the constraint reduces trading volume, improves trading performance (improves traders' market timing and reduces their disposition effect), and has little effect on the relative bid-ask spread. We complement these papers in three ways. First, we focus on cross-sectional variation in the relation between implied derivatives leverage and profits at the individual account level (skilled versus unskilled). Second, we perform high-frequency analyses of why skilled and unskilled investors over- or underperform. Third, we pin down the cause of leverage-induced underperformance (margin calls versus trading at unfavorable prices).8
  Margin trading in the Chinese stock market has attracted much attention in recent years. Hu, Liu, and Zhu (2019) study how (de)leveraging contributes to stock market quality using stock-level margin trading data. Feng, Lu, and Xiao (2020) claim that unregulated (shadow-financed) margin trading exhibits stronger explanatory power for time-series and cross-sectional asset returns than its regulated (brokerage-financed) counterpart. Taking advantage of both regulated (brokerage-financed) and unregulated (shadow-financed) account-level margin trading data, Bian et al. (2021) study investor behavior during the 2015 market turmoil in China. They show that leverage-induced trading can lead to contagion and thus affect asset returns and volatilities. Our paper complements these papers by considering the links between trader skill, leverage, and performance. Using account-level data, Gao et al. (2022) show that the complexity embedded in levered investment funds benefits sophisticated investors and hurts small and naïve investors. Utilizing a regression discontinuity design, Hansman et al. (2023) quantify the effects of introducing margined investing on the Chinese stock market bubble of 2010 to 2015.9
  It is worth reiterating what our Chinese context adds to existing research about finance in general. Our account-level intraday data from futures markets offer a unique opportunity to study the effect of derivatives leverage on the cross-section of trading performance. To the best of our knowledge, our paper is the first to directly document the heterogeneous impact of leverage and to uncover mechanisms underlying varying leverage-return relationships across traders. Our results indicate that the value of leverage for the typical investor is negative, in that it is associated with increased risk and lower performance, which suggests that regulating leverage may lead to improved outcomes.10For skilled investors, higher leverage is associated with higher profits, with the sources of superior performance due to de facto market making and traditional basis arbitrage. Unskilled investors' underperformance resulting from high leverage is related to the disposition effect and to the tendency of these investors to chase extreme returns, volatility, and skewness. The role of forced liquidations, and the sources of profits/losses due to leverage, are both novel issues that have not been explored previously.
  The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the Chinese futures market and our data. SectionIIpresents our empirical framework, related measures, and the method for identifying traders' skill. SectionIIIpresents empirical results for the entire sample, while SectionsIVandVpresent results for unskilled and skilled investors, respectively. SectionVIoffers results from DiD analyses around a regulatory change. Finally, SectionVIIconcludes.
article.I. The Chinese Futures Market and Our Da:
In this section, we first introduce the Chinese futures market. We then describe our proprietary data set.
  The Chinese futures market was initiated in October 1990. By the second half of 1993, there were more than 50 futures exchanges and nearly 1,000 futures brokerage companies across the country. In November 1993, the State Council of China issued a document titled “Notice on Stopping the Blind Development of the Futures Market.” By 1998, all existing exchanges were consolidated into three futures exchanges, namely, the Dalian Commodity Exchange (DCE), the Zhengzhou Commodity Exchange (CZCE), and the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE). The China Financial Futures Exchange (CFFEX) was established on September 8, 2006. All financial futures (three stock index futures, plus three treasury bond futures) in China are listed on CFFEX. The three other exchanges handle a variety of commodities futures.11
  Each futures investor (individual or institution; speculator or hedger) is required to open an account at a futures brokerage firm. Investors submit orders via trading clients to their brokerage company's trading counter. The brokerage company transmits these orders to the corresponding exchange where the product is listed. In China, futures exchanges are mutually exclusive because each product can only be listed on one exchange. This market structure implies that any policy change in one futures product will affect all investors who trade this specific product.
  Futures exchanges set different minimum margin requirements for different products; these are based principally on contract volatility. Take a product with a minimum margin ratio of 10% and nominal value of 10,000 yuan per contract as an example. Assume that an investor's initial capital (cash) is 10,000 yuan. After buying (selling) three contracts,10,000×3×10%=3,000$10,000\times 3\times 10\%=3,000$yuan will be locked up in the investor's margin account, with 7,000 yuan free cash remaining. The investor's leverage is simplyNotionalValueTotalAssets=NotionalValueMargin+FreeCash=30,0003,000+7,000=3$\frac{Notional\ Value}{Total\ Assets}=\frac{Notional\ Value}{Margin + Free\ Cash}=\frac{30,000}{3,000+7,000}=3$.12The margin ratio above is used to compute an overall portfolio risk level for each investor, and this level is used to issue margin calls, as we describe below. Further, all the required inputs for computing leverage (i.e., notional value, locked-up margin, and free cash) are clearly displayed on investors' trading platforms. It is therefore reasonable to assume that investors are generally aware of the leverage level they are taking on.
  Investors' leverage can change due to position changes, futures price changes, or cash injections/withdrawals. Holding futures prices constant, opening (closing) positions will increase (reduce) the notional value held and thus increase (reduce) leverage. Cash injections/withdrawals will lower/raise leverage. To consider the effect of futures price changes, suppose the price decreases by 20% (i.e., notional value per contract decreases to 8,000 yuan). The capital loss is(10,000−8,000)×3=6,000$(10,000-8,000)\times 3=6,000$yuan. The investor's leverage then becomesNotionalValueTotalAsset=8,000×310,000−6,000=8,000×32,400+1,600=6$\frac{Notional\ Value}{Total\ Asset}=\frac{8,000\times 3}{10,000-6,000}=\frac{8,000\times 3}{2,400+1,600}=6$. Note that after the price change, the locked-up margin and free cash are8,000×3×10%=2,400$8,000\times 3\times 10\%=2,400$yuan and 1,600 yuan, respectively.
  Our data set, provided by a futures brokerage firm in China, spans the period January 2, 2014, to December 30, 2016, and includes 733 trading days. While we were unable to persuade the broker to give us data for a longer period, our coverage compares with the samples of equity trades in Barber and Odean (2001), Grinblatt and Keloharju (2001), Barber et al. (2009), and Liu et al. (2022), in which the data span periods ranging from one year to six years. Our data set includes all clients that have opened accounts in the brokerage firm. The investor structure is similar to the aggregate Chinese futures market.15Our data set has the additional feature that we have time-stamped data on intraday transactions. Note also that all of our markets are in zero net supply, where buying and selling are treated symmetrically, and thus we are able to abstract from issues such as whether the period spanned a bull or bear market, which is pertinent to net long securities such as equities. In addition, our period was not unusual in terms of trading activity for the futures markets in our sample,16indicating no pitfalls in generalizing our findings.
  The data include six tables, which correspond to account details, capital, transactions, holdings, delivery, and entrust. The account details table contains the account ID, birth date, account open date, and account close date. The capital table contains investor-day-level information about marked-to-market profit/loss, as well as money injected and withdrawn. Each trading record in the transaction table contains account ID, transaction series number (reset daily), variety code (indicates the underlying asset), delivery month (indicates maturity date), trading price, trading volume, trading date, trading time (accurate to one second), trading direction (buy or sell), position change (open or close), a forced offset indicator, and the commission fee incurred by the trade. The holding table contains account ID, date, variety code, delivery month, day-end holding position (long or short), the corresponding day of the opened position, daily holding return, aggregate holding return, and the settlement price for both the current and previous trading days. The delivery table contains account ID, date, variety code, delivery month, delivery amount, delivery price, and the settlement price for the previous trading day. Although investors can submit market orders, limit orders, stop loss orders, and stop win orders through the trading client, all orders are submitted as market orders by the trading counter. Our data unfortunately do not provide details of the customers' orders; we only observe the execution price and quantity. The entrust table, which documents submitted orders, contains account ID, entrust series number (reset daily), variety code, delivery month, order price, order volume, order date, order time (accurate to one second), entrust direction (buy or sell), position flag (open or close), forced offset indicator, and a cancel flag.
  In total, the data set contains 39.4 million futures trading records (including futures deliveries) by 10,822 investors, of which 315 (2.91%) are institutional investors. In this paper, we focus on the trading records of the 10,507 individual investors.17Our data set includes 1,086 contracts written on 51 different underlying assets.18TableIreports summary statistics by type of underlying asset, including the number of trades (in thousands), notional value traded in our sample (in billions), gross profits (in millions) and net profits (in millions) earned by investors, the number of forced liquidations (at the investor-contract-day level), the number of investor-contract-day observations, the number of investors who had ever traded futures written on each particular underlying asset, and the average leverage level across all investor-contract-day observations. Futures written on the CSI 300 Index contribute the most turnover in notional value terms, and their investors also suffer the greatest losses. Futures written on Soybean Meal contribute the most observations. The sample contains 5,409 investor-contract-day observations that suffer one or more forced liquidations.
  TableIIpresents summary statistics for our variables across investors. The total notional value traded is 8.68 trillion yuan (about 1.33 trillion USD, or 0.9% of the market),19and the median turnover value is 41.9 million yuan. The aggregated gross (net) loss is 464.1 (998.1) million yuan, which indicates that more than half (998.1−464.1=534.0$998.1-464.1=534.0$million yuan) of the aggregate loss is due to transaction costs. The distribution of gross profits is right-skewed, indicating that there are a few individual investors who can earn high gross profits. However, net of transaction fees, the distribution of profits is slightly left-skewed. On average, at this single brokerage, individual investors lost 93,000 yuan, which, according to the data provided by the National Bureau of Statistics of China,20is higher than the per-capita Chinese gross domestic product (64,644 yuan) in 2016, and also higher than the total contemporaneous disposable income per capita of Chinese residents (20,167+21,966+23,821=65,954$20,167+21,966+23,821 = 65,954$yuan) over the period 2014 to 2016.
  TableIIshows that the mean (median) value of the average day-end assets is 162.8 (39.5) thousand yuan. More than 1% of investors have an average day-end asset value higher than 1.9 million yuan (US $300,000). The mean value of gross (net) profit per unit of notional turnover is about −14 (−15) bps, and the median value of gross (net) profit per unit of notional turnover is about −3 (−4) bps.
  To consider investors' holding periods, we define a “trading cycle” as beginning when an investor opens a position and ending when the trader fully clears the position. The duration of a cycle is the elapsed trading time between the beginning and end of the cycle. The row labelled “Duration” in TableIIreports the distribution of investors' median duration of trading cycles. For more than 25% of investors, the median duration is less than 16 minutes, which indicates that a considerable portion of investors conduct intraday futures trading. We also find that there are more than 100 high-frequency traders in our sample: for more than 1% of investors the median duration is less than 0.5 minutes.
article.II. Motivation for Analysis and Performa:
In this section, we first present a framework that motivates our empirical analysis of the link between leverage, performance, and skill levels of traders. We then describe our measures of performance, leverage, and trading intensity. Finally, we present our method for identifying traders' skill.
  The Typical Futures Trader. For the typical (average) trader, leverage, which magnifies the size of the bet, should result in higher volume, trading costs, and return volatility. If the typical futures trader's use of leverage is sophisticated, then overall returns should be increasing in leverage, net of trading costs, and vice versa. The impact of leverage on returns can arise from three sources:
  
  Our analysis first considers the full-sample link between leverage and returns performance, and then attempts to identify the impact of each of the above sources.
  In the remainder of this section, we describe our methodology for measuring returns and leverage, and we describe our procedure for identifying trader skill. In the subsequent three sections, we present our empirical analysis for the typical investor, unskilled investors, and skilled investors, respectively. Toward the end of the paper (SectionVI), we consider a regulatory event that increased statutory margin ratios for some contracts. Specifically, we perform a DiD analysis around the event to investigate the robustness of our regression results. We consider the event's impact on leverage, and consequently on returns gross and net of trading costs, for the typical investor, as well as for skilled and unskilled investors separately.
  The above procedure defines DRR, NTR, DTO, and DLV at the investor-day level. We can also define these variables at the investor-contract-day level. This can be done by simply recomputing NTR accordingly, and by modifying the numerators of equations (1) to (3) in a straightforward way. Thus, on dayt$t$, investori$i$’s DRR on contractk$k$is computed asDRRi,k,t=Daily_Profit(i,k,t)Cash_Injection(i,t)+Day_End_Asset(i,t−1)${DRR}_{i,k,t}=\frac{Daily\_Profit(i,k,t)}{Cash\_Injection(i,t)+Day\_End\_Asset(i,t-1)}$. Since, as illustrated in SectionI.A, the regulatory risk control of futures accounts is conducted at the portfolio level, we report most of our main results using observations at the investor-day level. Nonetheless, investor-contract-day-level observations are used in a few cases that we highlight below.
  Note that DLV represents the chosen level of leverage, not a mandated maximum level. That is, the numerator of DLV is also a proxy for trading aggressiveness, in that it measures how much of a nominal amount the trader is willing to put at stake. The denominator, of course, is a measure of total personal capital available, so that the DLV ratio captures the level of implied leverage. Our focus is on how and why a given DLV impacts trader performance, both unconditionally and conditional on trader skill. To prevent possible mechanical connections between ex post measures of skill, DLV, and performance, we identify trader skill out-of-sample, as described in SectionII.C.
  The row labelled “DLV” in TableIIreports the distribution of DLV (at the investor-day level). We find that the average DLV is 5.8, and the DLV exceeds 14.3 at the 99thpercentile, indicating that at least some investors tend to hold extremely leveraged positions. The next four rows of TableIIreport the distributions of NTR and DTO, unconditionally and conditional on trading. The unconditional mean (median) of NTR is 17.9 (5.4), and the conditional mean (median) is 19.6 (6.7). The unconditional mean (median) of DTO is 14.2 (4.9), and the conditional mean (median) is 15.7 (6.4). On average, more than 1% of investors traded 230 times per day. About 1% of investors have an average DTO greater than 160.22
  The last four rows of TableIIreport the distributions of gross and net DRRs. On average, traders earn negative DRRs. The unconditional mean (median) of the average gross DRR is−46.3$-46.3$(−23.7$-23.7$) bps, and that of the net DRR is−70.6$-70.6$(−39.1$-39.1$) bps. Both gross and net DRRs are left-skewed. When conditional on trading, both gross DRR and net DRR are more diversified with more negative sample means.
  Since implied leverage is our primary focus in this paper, we now define some alternative DLV measures, which are used for robustness checks.
  The second alternative is thelaggedleverage measure LagDLV, which is the one-day lagged version of ConDLV. Specifically, for each investori$i$on dayt$t$,LagDLVi,t=ConDLVi,t−1${LagDLV}_{i,t}={ConDLV}_{i,t-1}$. Note that although LagDLV addresses the reverse causality problem between the leverage and return measures, the lagged leverage measure may contain less information about the real leverage level for high-frequency traders who seldom hold overnight positions.
  To study whether leveraged investors exhibit persistent skill, we apply a bootstrap method proposed by Fama and French (2010) and Harvey and Liu (2022). In Fama and French (2010), the authors first calculate fundα$\alpha$’s using a three- or four-factor model (as per Fama and French (1993) or Carhart (1997)) on gross and net returns. They then compute zero-α$\alpha$returns for each fund via the fund'sα$\alpha$net of its monthly return. Finally, they use thet$t$-statistic on theα$\alpha$[t(α)$t(\alpha)$] from 10,000 bootstrap simulations of the zero-α$\alpha$returns to test whether actual fund returns have a nonzero trueα$\alpha$. Harvey and Liu (2022) further resolve the potential undersampling problem of the above procedure using a two-step method (see their section II.D). In the first bootstrap step, they find a bandwidthband̂(thres):=[tlower(thres),tupper(thres)]$\widehat{band}(thres):=[t_{lower}(thres),t_{upper}(thres)]$for realistict(α)$t(\alpha)$’s for each fund, wherethres$thres$is a threshold parameter determined by grid search. Then for each bootstrap iteration in the second step (as per Fama and French (2010)), they omit funds with bootstrappedt(α)$t(\alpha)$falling outside of their respectiveband̂(thres)$\widehat{band}(thres)$. We mimic the Harvey and Liu (2022) procedure to test whether leveraged investors' performance is due to luck (we setthres=2.4$thres=2.4$based on bootstrap simulations from our data set). We use the investor-day level DRRs in this routine, which require at least 12 return observations (with nonzero holding or trading). We compute investors'α$\alpha$’s after adjusting for five risk factors: the Chinese four factors (as per Liu, Stambaugh, and Yuan (2019)) plus an equally weighted average Chinese commodity return index.23We call this bootstrap approach the FFHL-procedure.24
  We define thep$p$-value (punskill$p_{unskill}$hereafter) for the FFHL-procedure as the proportion of the 10,000 simulatedt(α$t(\alpha$)s on zero-α$\alpha$returns that surpass the correspondingt(α$t(\alpha$) on unadjusted returns. Note that for top-performing investors, the smaller thepunskill$p_{unskill}$, the more unlikely investors' superior performance is due to luck. For bottom-performing investors, the higher thepunskill$p_{unskill}$, the more unlikely that investors' inferior performance is due to luck. In other words,1−punskill$1-p_{unskill}$is positively related to investors' skill. We can identify skilled (unskilled) investors by choosing investors with low (high)punskill$p_{unskill}$-s. We measure skill at the trader level, not at the contract level, which is consistent with Fama and French (2010) and Harvey and Liu (2022), who measure skill at the fund level. In the entire analysis conditional on skill, we measure skill in the first year of the sample and consider the link between leverage and performance in the remainder of the sample (as well as in-sample).
  There is a trade-off between “restricting the set of skilled investors to get a small Type I error rate” and “selecting enough skilled/unskilled investors.” In the remaining part of the paper, unless otherwise specified, we set the thresholds at 5% and 95% for identifying skilled and unskilled investors, respectively. That is, we classify investors withpunskill<0.05$p_{unskill}<0.05$as skilled and those withpunskill>0.95$p_{unskill}>0.95$as unskilled. For some critical results, we also use other skill measures/criteria to conduct robustness checks.
  Note that we have two DRR time series: gross DRRs and net DRRs. We use net DRRs to identify skilled investors, since truly skilled investors should be able to earn positive abnormal returns net of cost. We use gross DRRs to identify unskilled investors, since we want to eliminate the effect of transaction costs due to overtrading.25In this way, we identify 167 skilled investors and 1,292 unskilled investors using data in 2014.26
  TableIIIreports summaries for different groups of investors stratified by the skill measurepunskill$p_{unskill}$in a training period (2014), and also presents their performance and trading behavior in 2015 and 2016. Reported numbers include the cross-sectional averages (within each group) of each investor's time-series averages of net DRR (in percent), NTR, and DLV, the cross-sectional averages of Sharpe ratios associated with DRRs, the cross-sectional median of the median duration of trading cycles, the number of investors in each group, and the proportion (in percent) of skilled/unskilled investors. We find that the monotonicity in Sharpe ratios and net DRRs across sorted skill groups holds both in-sample and out-of-sample. The last two columns in Panel B show that the proportion of skilled (unskilled) investors almost monotonically increases (decreases) as the ex ante skill measure increases. Also note that in the out-of-sample period, skilled investors' Sharpe ratios are positive but insignificant, while unskilled investors' Sharpe ratios are strongly negative and significant. Thus, skilled investors do not overwhelmingly outperform out-of-sample in an absolute sense, although the superiority of their performance relative to unskilled investors does persist. While we continue to use the unskilled/skilled terminology in the remainder of the paper, a caveat is that our interpretation of these terms is relative, rather than absolute.
  TableIIIalso shows that the median duration of trading cycles exhibits a hump shape, that is, both top- and bottom-performing investors trade very actively: The median values of median durations for top groups are less than 10 minutes in both the in-sample and out-of-sample periods. Consistent with the duration of trading cycles, NTR exhibits a U-shape in both the in-sample and out-of-sample periods. In both periods, the variation in average DLV across different groups is relatively small and exhibits a gentle U-shape, indicating that both investors at either extremes of the skill spectrum tend to use higher leverage than other groups. Overall, these findings imply that investors' trading activity and DLV exhibit persistence.
article.III. Implied Leverage and Investment Per:
In this section, we analyze the relationship between leverage, volatility, and returns for the entire sample of traders. We then explore the impact of forced liquidations on performance. To obtain a first impression of the relation between the use of leverage and other investors' characteristics, TableIVsummarizes quantities of interest for investors grouped by their average DLV quintiles. We find that high-DLV investors tend to trade more and incur higher losses. Specifically, TableIVshows that DTO decreases monotonically from 34.2 for the highest DLV quintile to 3.9 for the lowest DLV quintile, the average net DRR increases monotonically from−109.3$-109.3$bps to−21.9$-21.9$bps, and the top DLV group also possesses the highest participation rate (in terms of the number of observation days) as well as the highest NTR. Thus, in general, the traders with the highest leverage are also the most active traders.
  To get a first impression about the relation between investors' returns and their implied leverage, in Figure1, we plot equally weighted average returns for each leverage bin. The upper plots in Figure1show that, across all investors, higher leverage is associated with lower gross and net DRRs. The middle panels indicate that, for unskilled investors, the negative relationship between leverage and returns is more prominent. The bottom plots in Figure1suggest that skilled investors may have skill to take advantage of implied leverage: Their average gross and net DRRs are the highest for the tenth leverage bin. We postpone the study of unskilled and skilled investors to SectionsIVandV, focusing first on the performance across all investors.
  We conduct panel regressions of investors' gross and net DRR on our leverage proxy, DLV. We control for NTR together with the notional-value-weighted average time-to-maturity and the notional-value-weighted average intraday volatility (computed using one-minute returns). We include the NTR control because Grinblatt and Keloharju (2009) propose that trading frequency can be related to sensation-seeking and thus impact performance.27Note that NTR is not directly related to DLV, which helps isolate the effect of DLV on returns. TableVreports the results with investor fixed effects and day fixed effects. The second and fifth columns of TableVshow that leverage is negatively associated with returns.28Interestingly, the third and sixth columns indicate that the NTR is positively related to futures investors' returns.29The fourth and seventh columns of TableVshow that, after controlling for NTR, a one-unit increase in leverage implies a decrease in investors' daily gross (net) return of 6.12 (7.94) bps.30The magnitude is large: Assuming that similar leverage applies each day within a year, the accumulated underperformance in terms of annualized gross (net) returns is about 14.9% (19.4%) per additional unit of leverage.31
  Furthermore, the magnitudes of the coefficients in the first row of Panel B in TableVare larger than their counterparts in Panel A, which indicates a positive relationship between trading costs and leverage. Specifically, if leverage increases by one unit, the induced transaction cost is about1.82(=7.94−6.12)$1.82\ (=7.94-6.12)$bps, or about 4.44% per year. The effect is large: For an average investor with a leverage level of six, the annualized leverage-induced transaction cost is as high as 26.6%. These results also indicate that the leverage-induced transaction cost accounts for about 22.9% (1.82/7.94) of the leverage effect on net DRRs. Note that without the NTR control, transaction costs account for an even larger share of the losses due to leverage—3.1 (6.8−3.7$6.8-3.7$) bps, which is about 45% of the total effect of DLV on net DRR. These findings confirm that high leverage increases trading positions, and hence should increase trading costs.32
  We next use the two alternative measures of DLV, that is, LagDLV and ConDLV, in SectionII.B.1to eliminate confounding effects arising from potential joint determination of DRR and DLV. The results, shown in TablesIA.IVandIA.V, are consistent with those in TableV.33Accordingly, hereafter we use the main measure of DLV; however, all reported results are robust to the alternative DLV measures in SectionII.B.1.
  High leverage is usually accompanied by high risk, and should increase portfolio return volatility. To investigate this conjecture, we conduct cross-sectional regressions of the standard deviation of investors' gross and net DRR on leverage. TableVIreports the results.34We find from column (1) that a one-unit increase in leverage implies a 0.73% increase in the daily standard deviation of investors' gross DRRs, equivalent to an 11.2% increase in annualized volatility. Column (2) shows that NTR also varies positively with standard deviations of investors' gross DRRs, and the effect mostly stems from using leverage (DLV): columns (3) and (4) of TableVIshow that the orthogonal part of NTR with respect to DLV has only weak explanatory power for the standard deviation of investors' gross DRRs. The results for net DRR reported in Panel B of TableVIare similar. Overall, TableVIconfirms that higher leverage leads to higher return volatility.
  Intuitively, leverage is an amplifier for portfolio returns, and it should have symmetric effects when investments make or lose money (i.e., increase volatility). In SectionIII.A, however, we find that leverage is associated with reduced performance even gross of commissions. In part, this may be due to forced liquidations (or fire sales) that accompany margin calls, as described in SectionI.A. Investors may find it challenging to recover from these intraday liquidation events. The effect of such events on levered traders' performance has not yet been explicitly explored in previous work.
  Panel A of TableVII, which reports the likelihood of forced liquidations conditional on DLV, indicates that the higher the effective leverage, the higher the probability of being mandatorily liquidated within a trading day. We find that for an average investor using a leverage level at about 10 (i.e., within the highest DLV quintile), the probability of suffering forced liquidations is 1.29%. For skilled (unskilled) investors using a leverage about 11 (10), their probability of suffering forced liquidations is 0.51% (1.74%). For high levels of leverage, the difference in the probability of forced liquidation between skilled and unskilled investors is statistically significant.
  Panel B of TableVIIpresents results from a linear probability model.35We find that for the average investor, a one-unit increase in DLV increases the probability of forced liquidation by 0.158%. A one-unit increase in DLV increases the probability of suffering forced liquidations by 0.047% (0.204%) for skilled (unskilled) investors. The regression coefficients in Panel B of TableVIIindicate that, for an investor (with NTR equal to five) using a leverage level of 10, the probability of suffering forced liquidations is0.158×10−0.396×ln(5)+0.211=1.15%$0.158\times 10-0.396\times \ln (5)+0.211=1.15\%$. This latter estimate is consistent with the results reported in Panel A of TableVII. Panel C of TableVIIreports summaries of DLV and gross DRR across observations with forced liquidations. The mean gross DRR is−27.31%$-27.31\%$, which, multiplied by the coefficient on DLV of 0.158% in Panel B of the same table, implies a reduction of 4.31 bps in average investors' gross DRR.
  The main cost incurred by a trader during a forced liquidation is the immediate price impact of the “fire sale” (or purchase). To address this issue, we conduct an event study around investors' forced trades. Figure2shows returns on volume-weighted average prices (VWAP) around investors' forced buys and sells separately. Figure2presents evidence supporting the liquidity (or price pressure) effects of forced liquidations. We find that forced sell trades suffer more severe liquidity costs than forced buys, which is consistent with Brennan et al. (2012). Quantitatively, forced sell trades bear a 50.6 bps liquidity cost within a 60-minute period, while the corresponding number for forced buy trades is 19.3 bps. These empirical findings are consistent with the notion of predatory trading proposed by Brunnermeier and Pedersen (2005), who argue that “if one trader needs to sell, others also sell,” which then results in a less liquid market and a shrinking liquidation value for the distressed investor.
  The rows “LiqCost90min” and “LiqCost120min” in Panel C of TableVIIare computed similarly to “LiqCost60min” but with liquidity cost estimated 90 minutes and 120 minutes after the forced trades (see the caption of Figure2). Our results are robust since the relative differences between the numbers in each of the last two rows and the corresponding numbers reported in the row “LiqCost60min” amount to less than 5%.
article.IV. Unskilled Investors:
In this section, we investigate what types of trading patterns contribute to the relatively unskilled investors' losses. That retail investors are subject to trading biases is well known. For example, they tend to exhibit a disposition effect, treat trading as a form of entertainment, and naïvely extrapolate from past returns (see, for example, Odean (1998), Liu et al. (2022), and Barberis et al. (2018)). The issue we consider is how leverage interacts with these biases.
  In TableVIII, we group the 1,292 unskilled investors bypunskill$p_{unskill}$and report summaries of the Sharpe ratios of net DRR, average net DRR (in percent), and average DLV. We find that even within the group of unskilled investors, the behavior and performance exhibit persistence: in both in- and out-of-sample periods, the most unskilled group (punskill⩾99.99%$p_{unskill}\geqslant 99.99\%$) has the most negative Sharpe ratio and the most negative net DRR. The results indicate that unskilled investors suffer investment losses consistently.36
  In TableIX, we present the gross and net DRRs for unskilled investors, sorted by DLV quintiles, both in- and out-of-sample. We find that in the year of identification, both gross and net DRRs decline with DLV, and the differences across the extreme quintiles are statistically significant.37In the out-of-sample period, the group with the lowest leverage earns positive gross and net DRRs, and all other groups suffer significant investment losses. In the last two columns of TableIX, we report the average NTR per day as well as the proportion of trading days (over trading days plus holding days). We find that the group with the highest leverage is the most active group in terms of the NTR per day, and that unskilled investors traded more actively out-of-sample.
  It is of interest to understand what contributes to unskilled investors' losses. We consider three important biases studied in earlier literature: extrapolation (see, for example, Barberis et al. (2018)), lottery preferences (see, for example, Kumar (2009)), and the disposition effect (see, for example, Odean (1998)).
  Barberis et al. (2018) propose that many investors form their demand for risky assets by extrapolating past returns. We investigate whether unskilled investors in futures markets also exhibit such tendencies. TableXreports results from panel regressions of unskilled investors' order imbalance (OIB) on past returns over periods spanning one minute to 20 days. We find evidence supporting trend-chasing at the daily frequency for the in-sample period (Panel A1), which becomes weak in the out-of-sample period (Panel A2). This finding, together with the results reported in Panels A and B of TableIA.IXat the daily frequency, nonetheless provides suggestive evidence of extrapolation. At the intraday frequency (Panels B1 and B2 in TableX), we find evidence of contrarian trading.38In fact, the upper panel of Figure3, which plots returns around unskilled investors' open trades, is consistent with the notion that unskilled investors try to time rebounds after big price changes, but such timing is ineffective.
  Using data from a nationwide survey of Chinese retail investors, Liu et al. (2022) find that the tendencies to chase extreme returns, volatility, and skewness are key factors in explaining investors' trading motives. We thus measure the maximum return, return volatility, and return skewness; see, for example, Kumar (2009) and Bali, Cakici, and Whitelaw (2011).39For each dayt$t$, these three measures are computed based on daily return time series across days[t−20,t−1]$[t-20,t-1]$. TableXIreports the results of panel regressions of unskilled investors' DRR on DLV and lottery preference measures for the out-of-sample period.40To investigate the effect of leverage, we also include interaction terms between these measures and an HDLV dummy (one if DLV is higher than the sample median, and zero otherwise).
  The results in Panels A and B of TableXIshow that when investors use high leverage, their gross and net DRRs are negatively related to extreme returns, volatility, and return skewness during the previous 20-day period. Interestingly, the baseline relations between lottery measures and DRRs are positive and largely insignificant. This indicates that unskilled investors pay for lottery preferences when their leverage is relatively high.
  Odean (1998) studies stock investors' susceptibility to the disposition effect by using two measures, namely, PGR (proportion of gains realized) and PLR (proportion of losses realized), at the daily frequency. However, investors in futures markets conduct high-frequency intraday trading, and they usually trade and hold contracts on the same underlying asset with the same maturity, which makes it challenging to use PGR and PLR. We thus test another implication of the disposition effect: Investors tend to hold losing portfolios for long periods and thus suffer more severe investment losses. TableXIIreports summary statistics on durations of trading cycles (Panel A) and absolute net returns (Panel B) for unskilled investors, separately for winning and losing cycles. To uncover the effect of leverage, we also separate all observations into two groups by DLV: The high (low) DLV group includes observations with above- (below-) median DLV. Sample means for different intersections are reported in TableXII. Differences in means between the relevant groups are reported together with the correspondingt$t$-values for testing the null of no difference.
  The results in Panel A of TableXIIshow that, on average, unskilled investors' losing cycles are significantly longer than winning cycles. Panel B of TableXIIshows that unskilled investors tend to lose more in losing cycles than they earn in winning cycles. These are supportive of a disposition effect. Another interesting finding from Panel B is that the difference between losing cycles and winning cycles is higher for high-DLV groups than for low-DLV groups, which is consistent with our earlier finding that investors pay for leverage. We find from TableIA.Xthat skilled investors are not subject to the disposition effect; their mean duration of losing cycles is significantly shorter than that of winning cycles, especially when they use high leverage.41
article.V. Skilled Investors:
We now turn to the other side of the coin: Do skilled investors benefit from derivatives leverage? If so, what is the source of their leverage-induced superior performance?
  TableXIIIprovides some characteristics for the 167 skilled investors identified as per SectionII.C.1, in- and out-of-sample. In this table, we sort (in descending order) these investors by theirpunskill$p_{unskill}$of net DRR in 2014, and report annualized Sharpe ratios of net DRR, average net DRR (in bps), and average DLV. We find from Panel A that the average Sharpe ratios of net DRR range from 1.60 to 6.75, and that the average net return ranges from 40 bps to 126 bps per day. The most skilled (punskill<0.01%$p_{unskill}<0.01\%$) investors have the highest average Sharpe ratios, net DRR, and DLV. Interestingly, the DLV levels of the skilled investors are very close to those of the unskilled investors (see TableVIII). The results for the out-of-sample period indicate that the most skilled investors identified in 2014 (punskill<0.01%$p_{unskill}<0.01\%$) also performed well in 2015 and 2016: Their average net return is 42 bps per day, and the average Sharpe ratio is about 2.8. However, there are only nine investors in this group. This confirms that within the group of skilled investors, very few demonstrate compelling evidence of absolute skill out-of-sample, although, as TableIIIindicates, relative skill differentials across unskilled and skilled investors do persist.
  To examine how skilled investors earn profits by taking advantage of leverage, we perform a dependent double sort (first sorting by NTR) on NTR and leverage (DLV), and report skilled investors' average returns in double-sorted bins in Figure4. This figure shows that skilled investors obtain higher gross DRR by trading more actively and using higher leverage. Interestingly, even skilled investors suffer from transaction costs induced by high trading activity: The second highest bar in the upper plot (located in the highest NTR decile) becomes negative in the bottom plot of Figure4. Leverage seems to facilitate a significant part of skilled performance: The first five most prominent bars in the bottom plot of Figure4are located in the highest leverage quintile.
  We formally study the relation between leverage, trading, and performance for skilled investors by conducting panel regressions of DRR on DLV, using a continuous skill measure (i.e.,Topc=1−punskill$\text{Top}_c=1-p_{unskill}$), a dummy variable (i.e.,Top5=1$\text{Top}_5=1$for skilled investors and 0 otherwise), and their interactions with DLV and NTR. TableXIVreports the results. Here, bothTopc$\text{Top}_c$andTop5$\text{Top}_5$are defined ex ante since we identify skilled investors using data in 2014, and TableXIVonly includes observations in 2015 and 2016. For comparison, we also report results without the Top dummy in the first column of each panel.
  We find that the coefficients on DLV and NTR in the regression results reported in TableXIVare qualitatively similar to those in TableV(the results in the first columns of the panels in TableXIVare different from the second columns of the corresponding panels in TableVbecause we only use data for 2015 and 2016 in TableXIV). The second columns of Panels A and B in TableXIVshow that the higher investors' skill, the less they pay for leverage. The third columns of Panels A and B indicate that skilled investors earn superior returns via leverage: The coefficient on DLV×$\times$Top implies that a one-unit leverage increase implies a 19.4 (16.6) bps increase in gross (net) DRR compared to other investors, which indicates that skilled investors earn about 40% higher annual net returns relative to others per unit of additional leverage.
  Note that the coefficients on DLV and DLV×$\times$Top5$_5$in the penultimate column of TableXIVimply that the net effect of using one leverage unit on skilled investors' net DRR is 9.1 bps (about 22% annually). The results are qualitatively similar if we use more strict criteria for choosing skilled investors (i.e., identify those withpunskill<1%$p_{unskill}<1\%$as skilled and use the corresponding dummy variable Top1$_1$); please refer to the last columns of TableXIVfor more details.
  It is of interest to understand how skilled investors profit from the leverage implied by futures positions. Such leverage can benefit traders along many dimensions. For example, Black (1975) suggests that informed traders can benefit from leverage, Adrian, Etula, and Muir (2014) suggest that leverage can enhance the efficacy of de facto market making, and Hugonnier and Prieto (2015) suggest that arbitrage activity can be more effective with higher leverage. So the question is how leverage impacts these activities. To address this issue, we conduct several formal tests.
  We first examine whether skilled investors tend to open long (short) futures positions when they are under- (over-) priced, using the futures-spot basis.42Since estimating theoretical values for commodity futures is difficult owing to the challenge in estimating convenience yield, here we focus on financial futures. We define the basis as the difference between market quotes and their corresponding theoretical estimates:Basist,T=Ft,T−St×e(rt,T−dt,T)(T−t)$Basis_{t,T}=F_{t,T}-S_t\times e^{(r_{t,T}-d_{t,T})(T-t)}$, whereFt,T$F_{t,T}$is the timet$t$market quote for a futures contract maturing at timeT$T$,rt,T$r_{t,T}$is the risk-free rate,43anddt,T$d_{t,T}$is the dividend rate.44FigureIA.2depicts the distribution ofBasist,T$Basis_{t,T}$at the minute frequency. The top plot shows the rawBasist,T$Basis_{t,T}$, and the bottom plot shows the basis relative to the theoretical value, that is,Basist,TSt×e(rt,T−dt,T)(T−t)$\frac{Basis_{t,T}}{S_t\times e^{(r_{t,T}-d_{t,T})(T-t)}}$. The figure shows thatBasist,T$Basis_{t,T}$is negative in more than 80% of the cases. As a matter of fact, the median raw (relative) basis is−65.52$-65.52$index points (−2.04%$-2.04\%$), which is consistent with strict spot constraints on short sales.45
  Since skilled investors' daily OIB is trivially different from zero across all observations, we conduct the analyses of arbitrage strategies at the one-minute frequency. TableXVreports results from panel regressions relating changes in the absolute basis to skilled investors' OIB. To reveal the effect of leverage, we also include DLV and its interaction term with OIB as independent variables. For the out-of-sample period, we find that skilled investors' OIB is higher after the basis widens, and OIB positively predicts a narrowing of the basis in the following minutes. The results are weaker for the in-sample period in the sense that the results hold only when skilled investors use higher leverage. This finding accords with the time-series plot of the absolute basis in FigureIA.3, which shows that the basis tends to be wider (and thus the strategy is potentially more profitable) during the out-of-sample period. Overall, we find supportive evidence that skilled investors can earn rents by conducting arbitrage strategies.
  To shed light on whether skilled investors' net buying can predict futures market movements, in TableXVIwe report results from panel regressions of holding-period returns over several intraday horizons on OIB.47We find that skilled investors tend to buy when previous-minute and contemporaneous returns are negative, and their OIB significantly and positively predicts returns in the following minutes. While the predictability of future returns from OIB accords with informed trading, such trading does not account for the negative relation of skilled investors' OIB with past returns. Indeed, such a pattern indicates that skilled investors step in to provide liquidity when needed. Specifically, the negative (positive) signs on returns prior (after) measurement of the OIB is consistent with skilled investors selling at the ask and buying at the bid. Thus, their behavior is consistent with de facto market making, or intraday liquidity provision.48The coefficients on the interaction term show that higher leverage further strengthens the tendency for de facto market making.49
  To provide more detail, in the lower panel of Figure3, we plot average VWAP returns around skilled investors' open buys and sells. Across all buy (sell) open trades of skilled investors, the average return across minutes−5$-5$through−1$-1$is significantly negative (positive). The results therefore provide a consistent picture that skilled investors are adept at liquidity provision.50
article.VI. Regulatory Increases in Required Mar:
In an effort to curb excessive speculation and volatility in commodity futures, the three major commodity futures exchanges in China issued a series of announcements between April 5 and 29, 2016, warning of the risk inherent in futures. They also raised the margin ratio requirement for futures written on 16 underlying commodities.51This policy shock provides a natural experiment that allows us to confirm our earlier findings about the role of leverage in the cross section of investors' portfolios. We carry out our analyses by adopting a DiD design.52
  Before presenting the results, it is helpful to discuss how an investor would react to the policy change. Note that an investor who holds both affected and unaffected contracts at the time of the change53can decrease positions in either affectedorunaffected contracts to address the new constraints. This is because each of the two ways can reduce the locked-up margin and, based on the mechanisms detailed in SectionI.A, it is the overall margin that matters. In other words, we do not have a prior conjecture about the relative effect of the policy change on affected versus unaffected futures. We therefore consider the full set of contracts in the analysis below.
  One other issue is that regulators also increased transaction fees on eight products during April 25 to 28, 2016. The increases are modest in absolute terms, however, amounting to about 1 bp on average; see TableIA.XVIfor more details. Results in TableIA.XVIIindicate that there is no material difference in DLV, NTR, and the likelihood of forced liquidation across products affected and unaffected by the fee changes. Nonetheless, to ensure that our results are not due to the fees, we use two approaches. First, we construct the measure “Adj” DRR, which assumes the same transaction fee for the postevent period as for the preevent period, and examine the robustness of our results to this measure. Second, in establishing treatment and control investors based on preevent leverage levels, we use DTO as a secondary match, to ensure that both treated and control investors are equally affected by the fees. More specifically, we take investors with preevent mean DLV above the sample median as treated, and matched investors with the closest preevent average DTO among those with below-median preevent DLV as controls. We require the relative difference between the average preevent DTOs in each matched pair to be less than 20%. Only matched treated and corresponding control investors (1,384 matched pairs) are included in the following analyses.
  Panel A of TableXVIIpresents DiD results for all matched pairs.54We include investor age and account age as controls. We find that the treatment effect on DLV is−1.79$-1.79$(with at$t$-value greater than 10). The treatment effect on “Force” indicates that the likelihood of forced liquidations is reduced by 0.5%, or by about 45% relative to the likelihood (1.1%) of the preevent period. The treatment effects on gross and net DRRs are significantly positive postevent for the high-leverage group, indicating that the policy change is associated with relatively better performance for traders with high leverage. The columns labeled “Adj” in TableXVIIreport results for “Adj” DRR, which are similar to those for net DRR. These results are consistent with our earlier findings that higher leverage is associated with lower investment returns and a higher probability of forced liquidations.
  Columns (7) to (9) in Panel A of TableXVIIpresent results of DiD analyses on return volatility (one observation of DRR standard deviation per investor, with errors clustered at the investor level).55The results support our earlier findings that the policy change is associated with reduced leverage levels for treatment investors, which should imply lower return volatility. For the treatment group in Panel A of TableXVII, the average total standard deviations for gross, net, and adjusted DRR during the preevent period are all close to 8.2%, and as a result the proportional treatment effects on return volatility relative to the preevent period are about 6.8%. The final three columns in the panel present results of DiD analyses on the Sharpe ratio of investors' DRRs. The results suggest that the implementation of regulations leads to a notable enhancement in investors' risk-return trade-off.
  We next investigate whether the policy change is associated with heterogeneous effects across investors with different skill levels. The policy change might protect the unskilled from losses while simultaneously impeding the ability of the skilled from receiving gains. However, the number of observations decreases considerably when we restrict our attention to the skilled, and, to a lesser extent, the unskilled (there are 36 skilled and 294 unskilled investors in the DiD setting). Linden and Samuels (2013) indicate that in such cases, it is desirable to conduct up to a 1:4 matching (four controls per treatment) to enhance the precision of the treatment effect and to avoid wasting control observations (see also Smith (1997)). We employ one-to-three matching, where each treated unit is matched with three controls, although a one-to-four matching leads to similar results. Panels B and C of TableXVIIpresent results of DiD analyses for skilled and unskilled investors separately. The point estimates in the panels suggest a lower DLV postregulation for highly levered skilled and unskilled investors. Panel B shows that the treatment effect on net DRR and adjusted DRR is economically large and significant. Specifically, the net DRR of highly levered skilled investors is nearly 50 bps lower per day, while that of skilled investors with low leverage remains unchanged. The policy change has a negligible impact on the return volatility of skilled investors. This effect, combined with reduced returns, results in a lower Sharpe ratio for skilled investors. In contrast, Panel C demonstrates that following the policy change, highly leveraged unskilled investors observe a significant increase in Sharpe ratio. Furthermore, the magnitude of the treatment effect on the Sharpe ratio is larger for highly leveraged unskilled investors compared to all investors reported in Panel A of the same table.
  TableXIshows that unskilled investors' return is lower when they trade futures with stronger speculative characteristics (maximum return, volatility, and skewness). We investigate the effect of the policy change on this tendency. TableXVIIIreports DiD analyses of unskilled investors' likelihood of forced liquidations, where we take observations with stronger (weaker) speculative characteristics as treatments (controls). Specifically, we define treatments and controls on a daily basis: Taking MAX as an example, on each trading day we classify investor-contract-day-level observations with a MAX higher than the sample median (across all products traded on the day) as treatments and others as controls. The results in TableXVIIIindicate that the forced liquidations become less likely postevent for treated observations.56This finding indicates that mitigating the tendency to speculate on lottery characteristics is a mechanism through which imposing frictions can reduce trading losses.57
  Overall, although the regulation has differential effects on the DRR of skilled and unskilled investors, the results confirm our earlier finding that, in aggregate, higher leverage accompanies higher portfolio risk and lower investment returns. Specifically, the empirical findings are consistent with the view that curbs on leverage might protect high-leverage investors: Their probability of forced liquidation is lower, and their investment performance is better, following mandated increases in margin ratios.
article.VII. Conclusion:
We all teach our students that derivatives trades imply levered control of notional spot market positions. But what does such implied leverage entail? Does the link between chosen levels of implied leverage and performance vary by investors' ex ante skill levels? How does leverage affect the sources of gains and losses for investors? And how do margin calls, and the ensuing forced liquidations, affect the leverage-performance link? Addressing these questions requires granular data for derivatives positions and performance. We make headway on these issues by using a unique data set on Chinese futures trading, which allows us to measure daily implied leverage as well as performance metrics in the cross section of investors. The data comprise individuals and institutions (although the majority are the former), and are available at high frequency, allowing us to ascertain intraday trading patterns as well. We use an identification period to measure trader skill. Our analyses indicate that while few individuals demonstrate evidence of superior absolute skill out-of-sample, relative skill differentials do persist.
  We consider the impact of implied leverage on the cross section of trading performance out-of-sample. In aggregate, leverage is associated with reduced trading performance and more volatile investment returns. We find that the use of derivatives leverage is associated with increased unskilled investors' losses due to lottery-like preferences for skewness, extreme returns, and volatility (Liu et al. (2022)), overextrapolation, and the disposition effect, and increased skilled investors' profits from intraday liquidity provision (Adrian, Etula, and Muir (2014)) and basis arbitrage. We explore, for the first time, the contribution of forced liquidations (induced by margin calls) to the relation between leverage and performance. For investors in the highest leverage quintile, the estimated liquidity cost due to forced liquidations is about 3.3 bps per day. The latter accounts for 5.3% of the highly leveraged investors' losses in terms of gross return.
  We conduct a DiD analysis around regulatory increases in required margin ratios. The policy change is associated with improved relative performance of the group with higher leverage, and this group's tendency to suffer forced liquidations is reduced. The performance of unskilled investors modestly improves after the policy change, while that of skilled investors deteriorates. Overall, the DiD results confirm our earlier findings about the negative effect of leverage on performance and the important role played by forced liquidations.
  One issue our paper does not touch upon iswhythe embedded leverage in derivatives positions exhibits cross-sectional variation.58Note that in the futures markets we analyze, leverage can fluctuate simply because of inattention combined with large price moves. It may also vary across investors due to differences in wealth levels or other account attributes. Ascertaining the sources of such variation represents an important area for future research.
